result: I’ve been thinking about the way light changes in late afternoon, how it slips sideways through the blinds and paints long, golden ladders across the carpet. It’s the same light that once landed on my grandmother’s kitchen table, turning the sugar bowl into a tiny crystal sun and making the peeling linoleum look like a map of some forgotten, precious country. I used to sit there after school, tracing those countries with a fingertip, pretending I could sail a toy boat along the bright channels and never reach the edge. Now, decades later, the same light finds me in a different city, on a different floor, but it still carries the faint smell of her coffee and the sound of a ticking clock that hasn’t existed since the house was sold. Time, it seems, is soluble in light; it dissolves and recrystallizes wherever the angle is right. I catch myself holding my breath, afraid the memory will evaporate if I move, yet eager to step into it and stay. Maybe that’s why I keep rearranging the furniture, angling chairs so the rays hit them like spotlighted actors who forgot their lines but remember the feeling of the play. The cat squints, yawns, agrees. Outside, someone’s remote-control drone hums like a lazy bee, and for a moment the twenty-first century feels polite enough to pause. I breathe. The sugar bowl is gone, but the sweetness lingers, unmoored, proof that even demolished rooms remain habitable inside the small, bright countries we carry everywhere behind our eyes.
